name: Slow Fade
description: Tests temporal decay — earlier sessions should fade while recent ones stay vivid. Stale client names should not appear in top results for current work.

messages.content: Just kicked off a new consulting engagement with Pinnacle Corp. They need help migrating their legacy Java monolith to microservices. Classic enterprise mess — the codebase is 15 years old.
Fifteen-year-old Java monolith — that's going to be quite the archaeology project. What's the timeline they're expecting for the migration?
They want a roadmap in 6 weeks and first services extracted by Q2. I'm working with their architect, someone named Priya. She's sharp — actually understands the domain boundaries already.
Having an internal architect who understands domain boundaries is huge for a monolith decomposition. Priya sounds like a good partner for this engagement.

Wrapped up the Pinnacle engagement. Ended up delivering a 12-service architecture. Now I'm starting with a new client — Lakewood Partners, a fintech company. They need a real-time fraud detection system.
Congrats on wrapping Pinnacle. Real-time fraud detection is a different beast — what kind of transaction volumes are Lakewood seeing?
About 10,000 transactions per second at peak. They want sub-100ms latency on the fraud scoring. I'm thinking Flink for the stream processing.
Flink's a strong choice for that kind of latency requirement. 10K TPS with sub-100ms scoring is ambitious but doable with the right architecture.

Done with Lakewood. The fraud system is live and performing well. New engagement now — Sequoia Health. They want me to build a patient portal mobile app. React Native, connects to their Epic EHR system via FHIR APIs.
Healthcare is a whole different world — HIPAA compliance, FHIR APIs, Epic integration. That's a meaty project. Have you worked with FHIR before?
A bit, yeah. The FHIR R4 spec is actually pretty well designed. The main challenge is Epic's sandbox being slow. The app needs to handle appointments, lab results, and secure messaging.
Those are the core patient portal features. Epic's FHIR sandbox can be frustrating but once you're past it, the production APIs are generally reliable.

Sequoia Health app is in beta! We got 500 patients in the pilot program. The appointment scheduling feature is the most popular — turns out people really hate calling their doctor's office. Lab results viewing is a close second.

500 patients in beta is a great start. Appointment scheduling being the killer feature makes total sense — it's one of those friction points that seems small but affects everyone. How's the Epic integration holding up?
Rock solid. The FHIR APIs have been reliable. We're adding prescription refill requests next sprint. The Sequoia Health team is really happy — they're talking about expanding to their other clinics.
Expanding to other clinics is a great sign. Prescription refills are a logical next step and should drive even more engagement.

queries.question: What project are they working on right now?
What technology is the current project using?
What's the status of their current project?
